Paragraph

As a result, if we denote by \(\phi\) the equivalence class of \(\{ c_{k}\}\text{,}\) it makes sense to define
\begin{equation*} \int_{c}^{d} \phi = \lim_{k\to\infty} \int_{c}^{d} c_{k}(x)\, dx, \quad \Vert \phi \Vert_{L^{q}[c,d]}= \lim_{k\to\infty}\Vert c_{k} \Vert_{L^{q}[c,d]}. \end{equation*}
Any \(c\in C[a, b]\) defines a Cauchy sequence \(\{c_{k} = c\}\) in \(L^{p}[a, b]\) and \(\Vert c \Vert_{L^{q}[c,d]}\) as defined through the limiting process above equals the definition through Riemann's integral.
in-context
